Just in: Hormuz shipping "nearly stagnant"! Goldman Sachs warns: Middle East oil supply recovery faces uncertainties.

Goldman Sachs Group said that if tensions escalate and shipping in the Strait of Hormuz is blocked, the recovery of Middle East crude oil supply may be slowed down. According to Goldman Sachs estimates, daily crude oil production in the Persian Gulf in June was still about 10.5 million barrels below pre-war levels.

Goldman Sachs analysts including Yulia Zhetkova Grigsby said in a research report on July 8: "Although Middle Eastern oil-producing countries have begun to restart shut-in oil wells in the past month, shipping disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz may slow down the recovery of crude oil production." "The recent attacks on many oil tankers highlight that the risk of navigation in the Strait remains high.

With the current ceasefire status unclear, shipping companies may be reluctant to pass, thus suppressing crude oil transportation volume in the Strait of Hormuz in the short term." Goldman Sachs analysts pointed out.

Goldman Sachs estimates that in the first 10 days after the Strait of Hormuz was reopened, crude oil transportation volume had returned to more than 80% of pre-war levels; however, after the recent attack on oil tankers, this figure has dropped back to about 70% of normal levels.

Goldman Sachs pointed out that Persian Gulf crude oil transportation and oil prices currently face two-way risks.

If negotiations can continue in the next 60 days, accompanied by shipping security guarantees and new sanctions exemptions, oil supply is expected to resume before the end of July; if negotiations are fruitless and attacks on tankers continue to escalate, oil flows will decline further.

Last month, as shipping volume in the Strait of Hormuz gradually recovered, Goldman Sachs, along with a number of investment banks, lowered their oil price forecasts, and its analysts also warned that crude oil oversupply may reappear.

THE VOL PICTURE

Wednesday's VIX rise was real, and that makes it different from everything else this week. VIX rose 0.77 to 16.90. Sticky Strike explained only 0.30 of it. The rest was genuine buying: Parallel Shift positive 0.25, the whole curve got more expensive. Put Skew positive 0.18, downside protection paid up. And the tell, Downside Convexity positive 0.25, the deep crash strikes got bought hard. That is Iran tail hedging. Upside strikes were sold to pay for it. Monday and Tuesday were mechanical noise. Wednesday was different. Traders paid up for real protection, and vol that rises for that reason does not come back down quickly.

At the same time the trailing tape went the other way. The biggest day in the trailing window rolled off Wednesday on schedule and 21 day realized dropped from 16.9 to 14.2 percent in one session. With VIX rising into that, the premium flipped from minus 0.8 to plus 2.7 in a single day. Options are now priced over the actual tape for the first time in weeks. Sellers of premium finally have cushion. On an ordinary tape realized falls to 12.2 percent by July 15 and 9.9 percent by July 22, and the premium builds toward plus 7 if VIX just holds here.

One warning on all of this. The falling vol math breaks if SPX has a single day that moves 1.5% or more in either direction, about 112 points. With the Iran situation, a day like that can arrive any morning. That leaves the market in a strange spot. Selling options finally pays well for the first time in weeks. But this is also the worst week to be short options if a war headline hits. Thursday's straddle at ±44 is the market pricing that exact tug of war.

Wall Street institutions such as Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs have recently warned that the international crude oil market may be at risk of oversupply next year. In the morning of July 6, Beijing time, the three major U.S. stock index futures collectively rose.

As of 7:20, Nasdaq 100 futures rose 1.45%, S&P 500 futures rose 0.5%, and Dow futures rose 0.04%. The precious metals market also rose slightly, with spot silver rising 1.2% to US$63.11 per ounce and spot gold rising 0.37% to US$4,189.54 per ounce. U.S.

stocks were closed for holidays last Friday, and the share prices of Korean chip giants rose sharply across the board. As of the close, SK Hynix rose 10.88% and Samsung Electronics rose 8.22%. In the previous two trading days, U.S. chip stocks fell sharply, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index falling by more than 11%.

The Goldman Sachs Trading Desk pointed out that the current decline in "U.S. momentum stocks" has provided room for short-term tactical rebounds - historical experience shows that the momentum factor often records positive returns during the correction phase.

However, Goldman Sachs reminded that momentum factor positions are still highly crowded, and if the deleveraging trend continues, the maximum potential retracement may still be twice the current decline.

Overall, the market is in the early stages of clearing momentum strategy positions, and the key to subsequent interpretation lies in the game between the liquidity environment and the rhythm of emotional repair.

Data from Goldman Sachs shows that the momentum factor has fallen by 24% since its peak, the largest retracement since the first quarter of 2023, which is significantly higher than the historical average retracement of about 12%. Looking at the adjustment cycle, the historical average lasts for about 24 days, while the current one lasts only 10 days.

Goldman Sachs pointed out that since 2026, high beta momentum strategies have experienced multiple losses of more than 10% within two days, and have since recovered to varying degrees. The current market trend is highly consistent with the previous model.

The bank believes that this round of amplified decline is mainly affected by structural factors such as low liquidity and weak summer trading, and is not a fundamental change in the fundamental trend.

Judging from historical experience, the winning rate of the bargain-hunting strategy has significantly improved after the Federal Reserve’s policy turns to easing (such as during the epidemic).

Goldman Sachs specifically mentioned that the market's doubts about the return rate on its capital expenditures caused by Meta's transformation to cloud business are similar to the path of impact on market sentiment caused by similar concerns in the past.

The bank's trading department currently judges that the core narrative of AI has not yet undergone structural changes that are sufficient to drive a deeper correction. International oil prices continue to fall International oil prices continued to fall.

As of 7:20 Beijing time, WTI crude oil futures fell 0.52% to US$68.33/barrel; Brent crude oil futures fell 0.61% to US$71.68/barrel. On the news, according to Bloomberg, OPEC+ has agreed to increase production quotas by 188,000 barrels per day in August.

This quota increase of 188,000 barrels per day will bring OPEC+’s cumulative new quotas since the outbreak of the war to 940,000 barrels per day, equivalent to nearly 1% of global demand. The above-mentioned production increase plan is jointly promoted by seven major member countries, led by Saudi Arabia and Russia.

According to previous reports by Bloomberg, OPEC+ has formulated a roadmap and plans to complete the full cancellation of the two rounds of production cuts in 2023 by September through continued quota increases.

The interim peace agreement between the United States and Iran has cleared key obstacles for the Persian Gulf oil-producing countries to resume exports.

According to tanker tracking data, oil exports from Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates have basically returned to pre-war levels, and the freight channels between the two countries through the Strait of Hormuz have been successfully opened. However, data compiled by Bloomberg show that the actual output of the two countries is still far below normal.

The current recovery in exports mainly relies on the digestion of early inventory, rather than the simultaneous recovery of production capacity. It will still take time to fully release output. At the same time, there are also variables in the situation in the Strait of Hormuz.

According to CCTV News, data released by the US-led Joint Maritime Information Center on the 5th showed that in the 72 hours from July 2 to 4, a total of 70 merchant ships were escorted by the US through the Strait of Hormuz. The scale of navigation was far lower than the average daily average of 138 ships before the conflict.

This data was released by the British Maritime Trade Action Office on the same day. The announcement shows that the daily traffic volume of U.S.-coordinated escort ships continues to decline: 33 ships on July 2, 29 ships on July 3, and only 18 ships on July 4.

There are currently two navigation channels in the Strait of Hormuz, namely the southern channel near Oman and the northern channel controlled by Iran. The traffic volume of the southern channel escorted by the United States has not continued to increase, and the actual total navigation volume of the northern channel is difficult to accurately calculate.

A股2026年7月8日（星期三），市场日度分析报告

一、当日行情总览

7月8日，A股市场呈现“冲高回落、深V震荡”的格局，三大指数集体收跌。截至收盘，上证指数报3970.88点，跌0.49%，成交额11923.56亿元；深证成指报14939.73点，跌1.87%，成交额13711.60亿元；创业板指报3845.35点，跌1.70%，成交额6517.74亿元。

科创50成为主要宽基指数中唯一收红的指数，报2016.23点，涨0.73%。北证50表现最弱，跌2.59%。大盘股表现优于中小盘，市场风格偏向蓝筹。

沪深两市全天成交额约2.58万亿元，较前一交易日缩量约159亿元，已连续5个交易日缩量，创下4月8日以来的最低成交水平。全市场仅1593只个股上涨，3790只下跌，超3700只个股飘绿；56只涨停、44只跌停。市场情绪整体偏弱。

二、分时走势回顾

早盘：四大股指集体高开。开盘后指数一度快速下探，创业板指、深成指均一度跌超2%，但随后展开强势反弹。科创50率先发力，半日大涨3.13%，报2064.20点。截至午间收盘，沪指涨0.52%重返4000点，深成指涨0.08%，创业板指涨0.95%。半日成交额1.72万亿元，较上日放量736亿元。算力租赁、半导体芯片、油气等方向领涨。

午后：指数冲高后持续回落，三大股指由涨转跌。机器人、锂电池、有色金属等板块加速下挫，拖累主要股指转跌。最终三大指数集体收跌，科创50涨幅也由超3%收窄至0.73%，呈现典型的冲高回落走势。

三、板块与行业分析

今日市场结构性分化极为突出——计算机、煤炭、油气等板块逆势走强，新能源产业链全线重挫。

领涨板块

1. 计算机（+2.44%）：涨幅位居申万行业第一，主力资金净流入94.46亿元。网络安全、国资云、算力租赁等概念爆发涨停潮，深信服、网宿科技收获20CM涨停，浪潮信息、广电运通、云赛智联等多股涨停。消息面上，金融业网络安全管理升级，金融信创市场规模预计2026年突破3000亿元；SemiAnalysis报告强调算力需求远未达到峰值。

2. 油气开采及服务（+3.50%）：涨幅居全市场首位，通源石油涨幅超7%。受中东局势再度紧张刺激，国际原油期货价格上行，WTI原油上涨2.76%至70.44美元/桶，布伦特原油上涨3.01%至74.16美元/桶。

3. 煤炭（+1.20%）：晋控煤业涨幅超6%。中信证券预测样本煤炭上市公司二季度净利润环比增长大约31%。

4. 旅游及酒店（+1.64%）：岭南控股、西藏旅游涨停，利好来自国务院原则批复《旅游强国建设“十五五”规划》。

5. 贵金属：招金黄金拿下5天3板实现涨停，背景为央行连续20个月增持黄金。

领跌板块

2. 人形机器人：绿的谐波跌幅接近16%，前期板块累计涨幅偏高，迎来集中获利了结。

3. 创新药：赛伦生物跌幅超过13%，板块前期持续上行之后开启回调阶段。

LIVE NEWS REPORT — THURSDAY, JULY 9, 2026
1. U.S. STRIKES IRAN AGAIN AFTER HORMUZ ATTACKS
The United States launched new airstrikes against Iran early Thursday after Iranian attacks on commercial ships near the Strait of Hormuz.
President Donald Trump said the attacks on shipping ended the fragile ceasefire. AP reported that U.S. strikes hit military sites and port facilities.
Iran responded by firing toward Bahrain, Kuwait, and Qatar, widening the danger across Gulf Arab states.
The Strait of Hormuz remains one of the world’s most important oil shipping routes. Any sustained fighting there can affect fuel prices, shipping insurance, supply chains, and military deployments.
What is still unknown:
The full damage from the U.S. strikes.
The number of casualties.
Whether Gulf states were directly hit or mostly caught in crossfire.
Whether talks can restart after the ceasefire collapse.
2. UKRAINE GETS A MAJOR AIR DEFENSE OPENING
President Trump said the United States will give Ukraine a license to produce Patriot air defense systems.
The announcement came during Trump’s meeting with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy at the NATO summit in Ankara, Turkey.
For Ukraine, this is significant because Patriots are among the most important systems for stopping Russian missiles and advanced air attacks.
Russia struck Ukraine again in the last 24 hours. AP reported Russian missile and drone attacks killed 4 people and injured dozens across Ukraine, including Kyiv, Kharkiv, and Zaporizhzhia.
Ukraine’s air force said Russia launched 169 drones. Ukraine said it intercepted 139 of them.
The attacks hit 15 locations.
Ukraine has also continued strikes on Russian oil and energy targets, including sites in Saratov and Tatarstan.
What may happen next:
Ukraine will seek faster weapons production.
Russia may increase strikes on Ukrainian infrastructure.
NATO allies are expected to discuss more Ukraine support before a July 13 Ukraine coalition meeting.
3. NATO SUMMIT ENDS WITH PUBLIC DISPUTES AND A UNITY MESSAGE
NATO leaders met in Ankara under heavy tension over Iran, Ukraine, Greenland, Turkey, Spain, and defense spending.
Reuters reported that Trump told NATO leaders privately that the U.S. wants to remain in the alliance.
NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte said the alliance had reunited after public arguments.
French President Emmanuel Macron said Europe has stepped up its defense commitments and needs greater strategic independence.
Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan urged allies to lift defense industry restrictions and pushed for more cooperation with Turkey.
Key numbers:
NATO has 32 member states.
Turkey says it is moving toward NATO’s 5% of GDP defense spending target by 2030.
Turkey also announced a $24 billion boost for its “Steel Dome” air defense project.
The main reality:
NATO is still publicly united.
The alliance is also under visible strain from Trump’s pressure, Europe’s defense demands, the Iran war, and Russia’s war in Ukraine.
4. UK LABOUR BEGINS REPLACING KEIR STARMER
Nominations opened Thursday in the UK Labour Party leadership race to replace Prime Minister Keir Starmer.
AP reported that Andy Burnham is likely to be the only candidate.
Burnham, the former mayor of Greater Manchester, needs support from at least 80 Labour lawmakers.
Starmer announced his resignation after two years in office.
Expected timeline:
Burnham could become Labour leader on July 17.
King Charles III is expected to appoint the next prime minister on July 20 if the process proceeds as expected.
Burnham has promised foreign policy continuity, including support for NATO, Britain’s nuclear deterrent, strong U.S. ties, and support for Ukraine.
The domestic pressure remains severe:
Slow growth.
Cost-of-living strain.
Public service pressure.
Party fatigue after Starmer’s short time in power.
5. GAZA PLAN MOVES FORWARD AS VIOLENCE CONTINUES
Reuters reported that Trump’s “Board of Peace” is planning a pilot humanitarian zone in Gaza.
The plan is designed to create secure areas for tens of thousands of Gazans with better services and aid.
Participation would be voluntary and vetted by Gaza’s new administrative structure and an International Stabilization Force.
The plan is moving forward even without a completed deal with Hamas.
Reuters reported that Israel intends to expand control over about 70% of Gaza.
More than 2 million people in Gaza remain affected by the war, damaged infrastructure, displacement, food insecurity, medical shortages, and security restrictions.
AP also reported that an Israeli strike in Gaza’s Sabra neighborhood killed Mohamed al-Wahidi, an Egyptian aid official who helped organize public World Cup screenings.
Three others were killed in that strike, including two children.
The Israeli military said it was targeting a Hamas militant and said it is investigating.
What is still unknown:
Whether the humanitarian zone can operate safely.
Who will fund it.
Whether Hamas, Israel, Egypt, and regional mediators will accept the structure.
Whether civilians will trust it.
6. DEADLY FLOODING IN SOUTHERN CHINA
Flooding in southern China has killed at least 39 people after days of heavy rain and Tropical Storm Maysak.
AP reported 26 people died in Hengzhou after a reservoir dam partially collapsed.
Nine people remain missing across Guangxi.
More than 130,000 people have been evacuated.
Rescue workers are using drones and about 5,700 boats.
In Guigang, more than 10,000 students and teachers were being rescued.
Typhoon Bavi is now approaching Taiwan and eastern China, threatening more rain.

The National Weather Service warned of severe thunderstorms and excessive rainfall Thursday.
Risk areas include:
Northern and central Plains.
Lower Missouri Valley.
Lower Ohio Valley.
Mid-Atlantic.
Main threats:
Damaging wind gusts.
Large hail.
Locally heavy rain.
Flash flooding.
Urban flooding.
The Weather Prediction Center showed a slight risk of excessive rainfall for parts of the central Plains.
NOAA’s National Hurricane Center reported no active tropical cyclones in the Atlantic, Eastern Pacific, or Central Pacific as of late Wednesday night into Thursday.

8. CONGO EBOLA OUTBREAK WORSENS
Reuters reported the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo has reached 600 deaths.
Reported infections have reached 1,759 people.
The outbreak was first declared May 15.
It was initially concentrated in Ituri, North Kivu, and South Kivu.
Now suspected cases have appeared in Tshopo province, including Kisangani, one of Congo’s largest cities.
One new case has a known link to the outbreak’s origin in Ituri. Another has no clear geographic link.
Health authorities are increasing surveillance, contact tracing, and containment work.
Reuters reported 51 new cases and 20 deaths in a recent 24-hour period.

The ruling upheld the long-standing understanding of the 14th Amendment.
Trump called the ruling a miscarriage of justice.

This affects immigration policy, birth records, families with uncertain legal status, state agencies, federal benefits, and future executive power fights.

Broadcom plans to invest $1.5 billion to expand its Fort Collins, Colorado factory.
The deal is expected to produce at least 15 billion chips.

It supports domestic factory jobs.

Microsoft is spending heavily on AI infrastructure while reorganizing parts of the company.
This is not just a tech-company story. It shows how AI spending is changing jobs, budgets, departments, and long-term career security.
Workers affected include people in gaming, business operations, engineering, design, marketing, and management roles.

Companies are investing enormous money into AI.
At the same time, some are reducing human payroll.
The practical impact is already being felt by workers, families, and local economies.

Reuters reported that Palestinians in Sinjil, in the occupied West Bank, are organizing night watches and WhatsApp alerts to respond to possible settler attacks.
Residents say they feel abandoned and are trying to protect homes, farms, and families.
Israel denies aiding violent settlers.
Settler attacks have escalated since the Gaza war.
This is a ground-level story of civilians trying to create their own warning system where official protection is disputed or distrusted.
13. HUMAN MOMENT IN GAZA: FOOTBALL, WAR, AND LOSS
A planned public World Cup screening in Gaza became a tragedy after an Israeli strike killed the Egyptian aid official helping organize it.
The event was meant to give civilians a brief moment of normal life during war.

The strike shows how even small moments of public life in Gaza remain exposed to military danger.
The Israeli military says the intended target was a Hamas militant and says the incident is under review.
For families in Gaza, the facts are simpler:
People gathered for a football match.
Children died.
The war remained present even in a moment meant for relief.
